title: Life Between Papers
subtitle: Every document tells the truth. None tells the whole truth
caption: To be issued a birth certificate that works, an adopted child must first have the true one sealed. A death, a birth, and the record no office keeps.
artworkAlt: A government triplicate form on a dark desk, headed Certificate of Extinguishment, with a red SEALED stamp
body:
Somerset House will not simply hand you your own beginning.

First there is a room. First there is a stranger: a counsellor the law insists must sit across from me before I am permitted to read the sentence that says I was born. She is kind. She has done this before. That is somehow the worst of it, that there is a procedure for this, a chair worn smooth by other people arriving to be told, gently, on the record, that they exist.

Then there is a fee. There is always a fee. You pay, in the end, to be introduced to yourself.
fullBody:
Somerset House will not simply hand you your own beginning.

First there is a room. First there is a stranger: a counsellor the law insists must sit across from me before I am permitted to read the sentence that says I was born. She is kind. She has done this before. That is somehow the worst of it, that there is a procedure for this, a chair worn smooth by other people arriving to be told, gently, on the record, that they exist.

Then there is a fee. There is always a fee. You pay, in the end, to be introduced to yourself.

And then, from somewhere behind glass and granite, out of the cold steel and the microfiche and the labyrinth of cabinets, they bring it up into the light: a small sheet, heavier than I expect, blue-black ink on paper the colour of an old tooth. My name is on it. Not the name I answer to. The first one. Jonathan Richard Jordan. Seven May, 1967. Eight-fifty in the morning, an upstairs room in a two-up two-down on Union Street, Southport, next door to a pub. Ten minutes later the same room was used again: my twin brother, arriving behind me into the same small square of the world.

Two beginnings, in one room, before breakfast.

Two names would be sealed that decade, not one. We were carried out of that first story together, under a single new surname, our given names left, mercifully, intact. Someone chose to keep those. I have since learned how rare that kindness was.

I keep coming back to one detail. Not the name. The margin. There, in a hand that was never mine, entered by someone whose face I will never see, is a single word.

Adopted.

Not a sentence. Not an explanation. One word, scrawled in the only place the state left for it, on the only paper that ever proved I happened.

I had gone looking for a record. What I found was a verdict.

---

Here is the mechanism, plainly, because plainness is its own kind of mercy.

When a child is adopted in this country, the original entry, the true one, the one made in the hours after the birth, is not corrected. It is not updated. It is sealed. The Adoption Order comes down, and the Registrar General enters a new record in a separate book, the Adopted Children Register, and issues from it a fresh certificate. That certificate is the one you will grow up inside. It goes to the school, the passport office, the registrar at your wedding. It works. It is, in every legal sense, sound.

It is also, in the places that matter most, invented.

The date survives, seven May, the one true thing carried across the border. But the room does not travel with it. The original address is not written on the adopted certificate; the house on Union Street simply falls off the edge of the document. New names arrive by order of the court. A whole prior history is not so much erased as withdrawn from ordinary issue, the way a form is retired: still filed somewhere, but no longer given out.

So the sequence, if you lay it end to end, is this. A death, administrative and quiet: the sealing of the first record, a death with no body and no funeral, the only one you are somehow required to outlive. Then a birth, legally impeccable and personally fictional. Death, then birth, in that order, both administered without ceremony, by people doing their jobs.

I was raised to believe a birth certificate was a fact about a person. It took me most of my life to understand it is a decision made about a person, usually while they are far too young to be consulted.

And the thing nobody warns you about, the thing that reorders everything once you see it, is that true and legal were never the same promise. They were handed to me in the same envelope, wearing the same ink, and I assumed, as everyone assumes, that they were one thing. They are not. I learned the difference at a counter, holding two versions of myself, neither of which had asked my permission.

---

Step back far enough from my one small sheet of paper and a larger shape appears, and it is not only mine.

Look at what the state actually certifies. It stamps the entrance. It will, one day, stamp the exit. Birth and death: the two hinges of a life, witnessed, numbered, filed. And between them, the entire thing, the only part any of us actually live: nothing. There is no form for the middle. No registrar issues a certificate for the years themselves. No office keeps a record that you were here in any sense richer than arrived and departed.

For most people this absence is invisible, and harmlessly so. The middle gets witnessed anyway, informally, unpaid. A mother says you have your grandfather's hands. A face in a photograph rhymes with your own. Family and memory do the registrar's work quietly, for free, and nobody notices there was a job to be done at all, because it was always already done.

That is the design. The state leaves the middle blank on purpose and trusts the family to fill it. It is not an oversight. It is the load-bearing assumption underneath the whole system: that somebody, somewhere, unofficially, is keeping the record of your life.

Which is a fine arrangement, right up until you are the person for whom that assumption was never true.

---

Because this is where the general absence becomes a private weight.

Take away the informal registrars. Seal the first record. Withhold the resemblances. Remove the face in the photograph that was supposed to rhyme with yours. Do all of that before the child has language to notice it is being done, and the blank middle the state left for the family to fill has no one left to fill it.

The job does not disappear. It falls. It falls onto the child, and the child grows up carrying a clerk's workload he was never told he had been given: to witness his own continuity, unaided, to be the sole keeper of the record that he counted, that the years were real, that the person at forty is continuous with the infant on the sealed sheet. Other people inherit their belonging. We are handed a stack of empty forms and a pen, and left to certify ourselves.

I have spent years filling those forms without knowing that was what I was doing. Every essay. Every reckoning. Every night spent standing in a bathroom studying my own face like a detective at a scene. It was all, I see now, the same act: testimony doing a registrar's job. Sworn, unwitnessed, unpaid, and absolutely necessary: the only method left to a person the record refused to keep.

Testimony has been issuing my certificates all along. It simply never occurred to me to call it that.

---

So let me call it that now. And let me say it to the two of you, Ella and Imogen, because you are the reason the naming finally matters.

No registrar was ever going to give me the document I actually needed. Not the one that says I was born. I have that, in two versions, and neither told the truth I was after. I mean the other one. The one that certifies not the entrance and not the exit but the middle: that the living happened, that it was mine, that the years between the two stamps were real and were spent and counted for something.

There is no office for that. I checked. So I did the only thing left to a person who has learned that authorship outranks any seal.

I wrote it myself.

This book is that document. Not a metaphor for it. The thing itself. A certificate of a life, drafted by the one person present for all of it, issued without a stamp because no stamp exists that could dignify it further than a name honestly signed. And it is addressed to you two, deliberately, because a certificate is nothing until it is witnessed, and yours are the only signatures I have ever actually wanted on anything.

There was, of course, one other witness to that room. My brother, ten minutes behind me, sealed in the same order, renamed in the same stroke: the only other living person who carries the exact shape of this erasure. And we barely speak now. The years the state declined to record did their own quiet work on us, and drew us to opposite edges of the one story we were meant to share. So his is not a signature I can ask for.

Which may be the truest reason yours are the ones I want. A life certified by the people you made, and kept, outlasts one signed by the person you began beside, and lost.

I want to be careful about how I say the last part, because it would be easy to hear it as a wound, and it is not. This is not the sad story of a man the state failed to file. It is a quieter, stranger claim: that the authored record is not a consolation prize for the official one. It is better. The registrar certifies that a birth occurred. I can certify what it was for. No cold cabinet can do that. No fee unlocks it. It cannot be sealed, because it was never held anywhere a state could reach.

They gave me a death, and then a birth, and left the certificate of a life undrafted, assuming, as they always do, that someone else would keep the record.

Someone else did.
beforeReading: This essay begins inside a single bureaucratic scene, the recovery of a sealed birth record, and works outward from it. It is not a legal complaint. It is an argument that the state certifies only the two edges of a life, birth and death, and that testimony, a book addressed to two real readers, is the closest thing an adoptee has to the record of the living in between.
originTitle: It began with a document that was true and invented at once.
origin: I kept returning to a strange fact about my own paperwork: that the certificate I grew up inside is entirely legal and, in the places that matter most, entirely made up, and that these two things were never in conflict. Adoption enacts a small sequence of forms, a sealing and an issuing, a death and a birth. I wanted to follow that sequence precisely enough to expose the form it leaves out.
meaningTitle: Authorship is not the consolation prize. It is the better record.
meaning: Writing this clarified that the book itself, addressed to my daughters, is the certificate of a life no registrar ever drafted. The state records that a birth occurred; it keeps no form for whether the life was lived. That form has had to be authored, unofficially and unpaid, all along. Naming testimony as certification, rather than as grief, is part of reclaiming authorship of a story that was decided for me before I could speak.
seoTitle: Life Between Papers | Adoption, Records, and the Life No Office Certifies
seoDescription: Every document tells the truth. None tells the whole truth. Jonathan Lyon on the paperwork of adoption: one birth record sealed, one certificate issued, and a life the adoptee must author himself. Companion to an interactive certificate artefact.

recordCard.kicker: Filed nowhere. In triplicate. Retain no copy but this one.
recordCard.title: Certificate of a Life

fields.label: Bearer
fields.value: the one who kept the record himself

fields.label: Born
fields.value: 7 May 1967, and every day since, on purpose

fields.label: Place
fields.value: an upstairs room; and then everywhere; and then, at last, here, with you

fields.label: Evidence submitted
fields.value: this. All of it. Every page.

fields.label: Issued by
fields.value: the bearer, at his own desk, unpaid, under no authority but the years themselves

fields.label: Witnessed
fields.value: Ella, and Imogen, on the day they sign

recordCard.footer: No stamp is available for this. Affix nothing. It counts anyway.
